Iran also said it had launched missile strikes on an Israeli government complex in Tel Aviv, as well as on security and military facilities in Haifa, in addition to an attack in east Jerusalem.
Day 4
Amid the rapidly intensifying conflict in West Asia, more than 780 people have been killed in Iran since the United States and Israel began their strikes on February 27, 2026, the Iranian Red Crescent reported.
Israel’s military said on Wednesday (March 4, 2026) that it had launched a “broad wave of strikes” on targets inside Iran, hours after the Islamic Republic fired three separate barrages of missiles towards Israeli territory.
Meanwhile, loud explosions were heard on Tuesday night (March 3, 2026) in Iraq’s Erbil and across Doha, Dubai and Abu Dhabi in the United Arab Emirates. Authorities in Dubai said the blasts were the result of “interception operations” carried out by air‑defence systems.
Saudi Arabia’s Defence Ministry said that an attack involving two drones on the U.S. Embassy in Riyadh caused a “limited fire,” following an earlier incident at the U.S. Embassy in Kuwait, which subsequently announced that it would remain closed until further notice.
Day 5
Despite the continuing U.S.-Israeli bombardment, Iran is “trying” to expedite the appointment of a successor to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei as Supreme Leader, state television reported on Wednesday (March 4, 2026).
Israel’s Defence Minister, Israel Katz, meanwhile warned that Israel would target and assassinate any Iranian leader chosen to succeed Ayatollah Khamenei.
As the conflict entered its fifth day, Israel carried out fresh strikes on Iran and Lebanon. Lebanese state media reported that a residential building was hit in the latest bombardment. In southern Lebanon, Israeli forces entered the town of Khiam as part of their ground operation against the Iran‑backed Hezbollah.
The Israeli military stated that Iran retains significant missile‑launching capability, even as Israel continues to target launch sites across the Islamic Republic. Separately, Mohammad Mokhber, a senior aide to the late Supreme Leader, said on state television that Iran has no intention of negotiating with the United States and is capable of sustaining the war “for as long as we want.
Meanwhile, a U.S. submarine attacked an Iranian warship, which sank in the Indian Ocean off the coast of Sri Lanka on Wednesday, bringing the West Asian conflict closer to the Indian subcontinent. At least 83 people were killed in the torpedo attack. U.S. Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth confirmed the strike at a press conference in Washington, D.C.
A senior Iranian official said that a public ceremony for citizens to pay their respects to the country’s Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, which was originally scheduled to take place at Tehran’s Imam Khomeini Prayer Hall, had been “postponed”, state media said.
Day 6
Aerial strikes across West Asia entered their sixth day on Thursday (March 5, 2026), following the sinking of an Iranian warship by a U.S. submarine and Tehran’s warning that it would retaliate against military and economic assets across the region.
Early on Thursday (March 5, 2026), Iran launched missiles at Israel. Israel confirmed the Iranian strike shortly after its military announced fresh operations in Lebanon, targeting Hezbollah positions in the southern suburbs of Beirut.
Meanwhile, a second Iranian warship was heading towards Sri Lanka’s territorial waters on Thursday, just a day after a U.S. submarine destroyed another Iranian frigate, killing at least 87 sailors.
Official sources said the vessel carries more than 100 crew members, raising fears that it could face the same fate as its sister ship, which was sunk off Sri Lanka’s southern coast on Wednesday.
In New Delhi, the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) dismissed claims made by former U.S. Army Colonel Douglas Macgregor that the United States is using Indian naval bases to attack Iran.
Amid mounting regional tensions in West Asia, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi warned that Tehran is fully prepared for any potential ground offensive, cautioning that such an escalation would bring “disaster” upon Iran’s adversaries.
Meanwhile, Israel’s military chief announced that he has instructed Israeli forces to widen the zone under Israel’s control in southern Lebanon. The expansion marks a further intensification of Israel’s ongoing operations against Hezbollah along the northern frontier.
In Sri Lanka, President Anura Kumara Dissanayake said authorities have begun offloading Iranian crew members from a vessel positioned off the country’s coast. “A total of 208 crew are on the ship, and Sri Lanka has started the process of offloading the Iranian crew. Once the crew is safely off‑boarded, the vessel will be moved to the Trincomalee harbour,” the President said.
Day 7
On the seventh consecutive day of the deepening crisis in West Asia, Israeli airstrikes hit the capitals of Iran and Lebanon, while the U.S. reportedly struck an Iranian drone carrier at sea, intensifying its campaign targeting the Islamic Republic’s fleet of warships
“UNHCR has declared the escalating crisis in the Middle East a major humanitarian emergency requiring an immediate response across the region,” Ayaki Ito, the agency’s emergency chief and cross-regional refugee coordinator, told reporters in Geneva.
At the same time, Iran launched fresh retaliatory strikes on neighbouring countries hosting U.S. military installations.
Two days after a U.S. submarine torpedoed and sank an Iranian warship off the coast of Sri Lanka, Iran warned on Friday (March 6, 2026) that such an attack on an “unarmed ship cannot go with impunity.” Meanwhile, India rejected claims that it had aided the U.S. operation, with a senior naval official calling the reports “baseless.”
Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump said there would be no deal struck with Iran except “unconditional surrender,” a week after launching war with Israel against Tehran.
On Thursday (March 5, 2026), Trump had said that he wants to be involved in choosing the next leader of Iran. However, Iran’s ambassador to the United Nations condemned U.S. interference after Mr. Trump insisted that he should be involved in selecting the successor of slain supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
“Iran is a sovereign and independent state. It does not accept and will never allow any foreign power to interfere in its internal affairs,” Mr. Iravani told reporters in New York
Day 8
After U.S. President Donald Trump said on Friday (March 6, 2026) that he would not seek a deal with Iran without the country’s “unconditional surrender”, Washington warned of a forthcoming bombing campaign that officials said would be the most intense of the weeklong conflict.
As Israeli warplanes struck the Iranian capital and Beirut, Iran launched additional retaliatory attacks on Israel and Gulf countries on the seventh day of the war.
Meanwhile, Indian airlines have flown back nearly 15,000 stranded passengers from the UAE, Saudi Arabia and Oman since the start of the week.
In Dubai, an explosion was heard near the airport, followed by a cloud of smoke, a witness told AFP, as Iran pressed its attacks across the Gulf.
However, the Dubai Media Office described it as “a minor incident resulting from the fall of debris after an interception” in a post on X, and also denied reports circulating about the airport.
In the first reported attack on the Iran’s oil infrastructure, U.S. and Israeli strikes hit an oil storage facility in Tehran on Saturday (March 7, 2026), triggering a fire, state media reported. Soon after, Iran’s Guards said they had targeted Israel’s Haifa oil refinery in response to the attack.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu also said that Israel would continue its war with Iran “with all our force”, and alongside the United States had almost total control of Iranian skies after a week of strikes.
Day 9
As tensions rise in West Asia amid the escalating war, the clerical body responsible for selecting Iran’s next Supreme Leader has reached a decision, members said on Sunday (March 8, 2026), although the name has yet to be announced. At the same time, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards declared that the country is capable of sustaining an intense war against the United States and Israel for at least six months.
Gulf states, including Israel, reported missile and drone attacks originating from Iran. Saudi Arabia said it intercepted a drone targeting the diplomatic quarter in Riyadh, adding that there were no casualties or damage. Kuwait reported that fuel tanks at its international airport were targeted by Iranian drones.
On Saturday (March 7), an Israeli attack hit an oil storage facility in Tehran, which sent up pillars of fire that could be seen in Associated Press video as a glow against the night sky. It appeared to be the first time a civil industrial facility has been targeted in the war.
Meanwhile, President Donald Trump blamed Iran for the deadly strike on an elementary school in Minab, which Iranian officials say killed at least 150 people. “We think it was done by Iran. Because they are very inaccurate, as you know, with their munitions,” Mr. Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One.
Day 10
Amid the escalating turmoil in West Asia, United States President Donald Trump said on Monday (March 9, 2026) that the conflict with Iran may be short-lived, even as the country installs a new hard-line supreme leader to oversee the fight for the Islamic theocracy’s survival.
Mr. Trump’s latest remarks came as the Israeli military announced that it had carried out a series of “broad strikes” on Tehran. Earlier, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said they would increase both the force and the frequency of missile launches and extend their range. “From now on, no missiles will be launched with warheads lighter than one ton”, state media cited the commander Majid Mousavi.
Mr. Trump dialled up his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin to discuss developments in Iran and Ukraine, the Kremlin said on Monday (March 9, 2026). During the hour-long conversation, described as “constructive and business-like”, Mr. Putin outlined several proposals aimed at bringing the conflict in West Asia to a swift conclusion. The two leaders also reviewed conditions in Venezuela in relation to the global oil market.
Meanwhile, France and its allies are preparing what President Emmanuel Macron described as a defensive mission to reopen the strategically significant Strait of Hormuz, as the war entered its second week. Oil prices rose above 114 dollars a barrel for the first time since 2022 as tensions mounted, raising concerns over production and maritime traffic across West Asia.
Day 11
Meanwhile, Iran launched fresh attacks on Gulf Arab countries, including the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, as it stepped up pressure across the region. Five pro‑Iranian militants were reported killed in an airstrike in northern Iraq.
On Monday (March 9, 2026), Mr. Trump warned that the United States would respond much more forcefully if Tehran attempted to halt the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz. The narrow waterway, one of the world’s most vital shipping routes, has seen an abrupt collapse in commercial traffic amid rising tensions in West Asia.
Although Mr. Trump repeated on Tuesday (March 10, 2026) that the war with Iran could conclude soon, he remained vague on a timeline for ending the attacks that have shaken West Asia and unsettled the global economy might cease. More than twenty heavy explosions were heard in Tehran in what was described as the most intense air raid on the capital since the war began on February 28. Israel’s military said further missiles had been launched from Iran towards Israel late on Monday.
The United States military said it had “eliminated” sixteen Iranian mine‑laying vessels near the Strait of Hormuz, adding that updated count comes after U.S. President Donald Trump’s earlier claim that ten inactive boats had been struck. Washington again warned Tehran against placing mines in the strategic corridor.
Also, fresh explosions were reported from Tehran late on Tuesday (March 11, 2026), hours after the U.S. Defence Secretary indicated that strikes on Iran were likely to reach their highest intensity as the conflict entered its tenth day.
External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar held a detailed conversation on Tuesday evening with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi on the latest developments in the West Asia crisis.
Meanwhile, the United States has urged Israel to halt attacks on Iran’s energy infrastructure, Axios reported on Tuesday (March 11, 2026), with the Trump administration reportedly citing three reasons, including a goal to cooperate with Iran’s oil sector after the war. Israel is also likely to allocate a “special war budget” of tens of billions of shekels for extra defence spending to finance the air war with Iran.
Day 12
On the twelfth consecutive day of the intensifying Iran–Israel war, Saudi Arabia said on Wednesday (March 11, 2026) that it had intercepted seven ballistic missiles in separate attacks aimed at an air base and its eastern region, as Iran renewed its fire on Gulf neighbours.
Israeli forces carried out fresh strikes on the southern suburbs of Beirut on Wednesday, Lebanese state media reported, with the Israeli military stating that it was again targeting the Hezbollah movement, which is backed by Iran.
In the United Arab Emirates, two drones fell near Dubai airport, injuring four people, the Dubai media office said. It added that air traffic continued to operate normally.
On Wednesday (March 10, 2026), United States President Donald Trump said that the U.S. had “won” the Iran war, but added that the United States will stay in the fight to finish the job. He said U.S. troops had so far destroyed fifty eight Iranian mine‑laying vessels, amid reports that Iran had begun deploying mines in the Strait of Hormuz. The mines had been placed “in the last few days” and most of their positions were known, Reuters reported, citing sources.
Iran said it was prepared for a prolonged war of attrition that it claimed would “destroy” the global economy. The statement came after Tehran’s forces fired on two commercial vessels and warned that ships belonging to the U.S. or its allies would be targeted.
Meanwhile, India expressed concern over the rising attacks on commercial shipping in West Asia, after a Thai‑flagged bulk carrier bound for Kandla Port in Gujarat was struck by Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
Day 13
As the conflict entered its thirteenth day on Thursday (March 12, 2026), an Indian national was killed in an attack carried out by Iran using a “suicide” boat against a United States-owned oil tanker near Iraq. U.S.-owned and Marshal Island-flagged oil tanker, Safesea Vishnu, was struck off the Khor Al Zubair port within Iraq’s territorial waters.
The attack prompted authorities to suspend operations at all Iraqi oil terminals. Officials said that rescuers had recovered one body and assisted thirty eight others following the attack.
Meanwhile, Israeli forces on Thursday (March 12, 2026) said that they had carried out a fresh and wide scale wave of strikes on what they described as terror infrastructure belonging to the Hezbollah group across Lebanon, along with drone attacks directed at Tehran. Soon after midnight, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards announced that they had launched a joint missile operation with Hezbollah against targets inside Israel.
Kuwait’s Ministry of Electricity said six power transmission lines went out of service after debris from intercepted drones fell on them. The Ministry added that the supply of electricity and water remained under control.
Iran outlined three conditions for bringing the conflict to an end, including firm guarantees against future attacks, even as the United States and Israel continued their air strikes inside the country. “The only way to end this war — ignited by the Zionist regime & U.S. — is recognising Iran’s legitimate rights, payment of reparations, and firm international guarantees against future aggression,” Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said.
Day 14
After a fortnight of escalating hostilities in the Gulf region, the Israeli military said on Friday (March 13, 2026) that it struck the Zrarieh Bridge over the Litani River in Lebanon, saying the structure served as a key crossing used by Hezbollah. According to the military, Hezbollah had recently placed launchers near the bridge and carried out attacks from there towards Israel. It appeared to be the first instance in the current campaign against Hezbollah in which the Israeli forces acknowledged targeting civilian infrastructure.
Meanwhile, Iran launched a series of attacks early on Friday (March 13, 2026) on Gulf Arab states, including dozens of drones directed at Saudi Arabia, following warnings from its new supreme leader about hosting American bases. United States President Donald Trump threatened further retaliation, writing in a social media post that “Iran’s Navy is gone, their Air Force is no longer, missiles, drones and everything else are being decimated, and their leaders have been wiped from the face of the earth.”
Dubai’s media office confirmed that a building in the city had been struck. Officials said the United Arab Emirates’ air defences had intercepted more than 1,500 Iranian drones and nearly 300 missiles during the ongoing Middle East conflict.“Authorities confirmed that debris from a successful interception caused a minor incident on the facade of a building in central Dubai,” the media office posted on X, adding that no injuries were reported.
In the United States, President Donald Trump on Friday (March 13, 2026) told reporters that the objectives of the U.S. and Israel with regards to Iran “might be a little different”. Shortly thereafter, he said in a post on Truth Social that the U.S. military had carried out heavy bombing of military targets on Iran’s Kharg Island, which handles almost all of Iran’s crude exports, and threatened that the island’s oil infrastructure could also be targeted.
Meanwhile, an Israeli strike on a primary healthcare centre in southern Lebanon killed at least 12 medical personnel, the Lebanese Health Ministry said, as fighting between Israel and Hezbollah continued. Israeli shelling also hit a United Nations peacekeeping base in the region on Friday (March 13, 2026), according to state media reports.
In Tehran, a large explosion rocked a central square where thousands were gathered for an annual state-organised rally to support the Palestinians and call for Israel’s demise. After Israel warned that it would target the rally area in central Tehran, Iran and the Israeli military launched fresh rounds of missile attacks hours after deadly explosions struck Tehran near a pro‑government rally attended by senior officials.
The U.S. State Department announced a reward of $10 million for information on Iran’s new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, and other top officials. In a mark of major U.S. troop movement, around 2,500 Marines and at least one amphibious assault ship were reported to be heading towards West Asia.
Also, two Indian nationals were killed in an attack in Sohar, Oman, the Ministry of External Affairs confirmed. In view of the situation in the Gulf, the Indian Consulate in Dubai announced a 30‑day extension for Indian visas and e‑visas, along with a waiver of overstay penalties for foreigners stranded in India.
Day 15
As the escalating conflict in West Asia entered its third week, Iran said that no oil infrastructure was damaged in U.S. strikes on Kharg Island, Iranian media reported on Saturday (March 14, 2026).
The Fars news agency, citing sources on the ground, said there was no damage to oil facilities, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump stated that American bombardment of the island had destroyed military targets.
U.S. President Donald Trump had earlier threatened in a social media post to target oil infrastructure on the island, a critical hub for Iran’s energy exports, if Tehran continued to block the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
On Friday (March 13, 2026), Mr. Trump said U.S. forces had “obliterated” military targets on Iran’s Kharg Island, the main terminal handling Iran’s oil exports, and warned that the island’s oil facilities could be targeted next. Earlier, the Speaker of the Iranian Parliament warned that any such strike would trigger a new level of retaliation.
Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump urged other nations to help secure a vital shipping lane choked off by the war with Iran that showed no signs of slowing on Saturday as strikes hit the U.S. embassy in Baghdad and a major Emirati energy facility.
Day 16
On the 15th consecutive day of the escalating U.S.-Israel-Iran war, U.S. President Donald Trump threatened further strikes on Iran’s Kharg Island oil export hub and urged allies to deploy warships to secure the Strait of Hormuz, as Tehran vowed to step up its response and the war showed no sign of ending on Sunday (March 15, 2026).
Meanwhile, the Israeli military said on Saturday (March 14, 2026) that it had killed two senior Iranian intelligence officials in an air strike in Tehran. Israel identified the men as Abdollah Jalali‑Nasab and Amir Shariat, describing them as senior officials in the intelligence directorate of Khatam al‑Anbiya, the Iranian military’s central operations command.
Iran, for its part, warned that it would target American companies in the region if its energy facilities were attacked in the U.S.–Israeli war against the Islamic Republic. Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said that the U.S. strikes on Kharg Island involved short‑range rockets launched from two bases in the United Arab Emirates, including one located close to Dubai.
The UAE has arrested and ordered an urgent trial of ten individuals for sharing misleading videos on social media amid rising tensions related to the Iran conflict. Dubai aims to uphold its reputation as a haven against Iranian aggression, with influencers backing the government’s narrative. Meanwhile, AI-generated deepfake videos on X show false scenes of American soldiers captured by Iran and U.S. embassies on fire.
The U.S. Department of Defense identified the six U.S. airmen who were killed in a plane crash over Iraq.
U.S. President Donald Trump urged allies to send warships to secure the Strait of Hormuz, advising evacuations at three UAE ports. A senior Japanese adviser stated that the threshold for Japan to deploy warships is “extremely high.” South Korea’s presidential office is reviewing Trump’s request for allies to assist in securing the shipping lane. Meanwhile, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards threatened to target Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu as the conflict persists.
Clouds of toxic smoke released into the atmosphere by U.S.-Israeli airstrikes targeting Iranian oil facilities are now returning to Earth as “black rain,” leading international health officials to issue warnings about significant public health risks. Meanwhile, Israel’s military announced it began a widespread series of strikes in western Iran, marking over two weeks of ongoing conflict between the U.S.-Israeli coalition and the Islamic Republic. Moreover, overnight airstrikes in southern Lebanon resulted in at least four fatalities, according to Lebanese state media and government sources, as Israel intensified its operations against the Iran-supported Hezbollah.
Meanwhile, Donald Trump warned that he is not ready to seek a deal to end the war with Iran, as U.S. ally Israel launched a new wave of strikes and Tehran’s Revolutionary Guards threatened to hunt down and kill the Israeli leader. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said that he expects the U.S. war with Iran to end within “the next few weeks,” with oil supplies rebounding and energy costs declining afterwards. However, Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Iran was not interested in talks with the United States, pushing back on U.S. President Donald Trump’s stance that Tehran wants a deal to end the war.
As the crisis escalated, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu published a short video on X making light of rumours on social media that he was dead. “I’m dead for coffee,” he said sarcastically on his official X account as he received a cup at a cafe outside Jerusalem.
The end of the day witnessed several drones and rockets targeting a military base at the Baghdad airport complex, which also houses a U.S. diplomatic facility, three security sources told AFP. Meanwhile, the World Health Organization said that it had released $2 million from its Contingency Fund for Emergencies (CFE) to support the health response in Lebanon, Iraq and Syria amidst the ongoing crisis in West Asia.
Day 17
Israel initiated a fresh series of attacks on Tehran as the US-Israel war on Iran entered its 17th day on Monday (March 16, 2026).
Tensions rose in the Gulf region as authorities suspended operations at Dubai International Airport due to a drone-related incident that caused a nearby fire. Dubai-based Emirates later confirmed it would resume a limited number of flights, although several scheduled routes for the day have been cancelled. An Emirates flight carrying 325 passengers, which was en route to Dubai, had to return to Kochi, Kerala, four hours after departing in the morning due to the unexpected airport closure.
Minister of External Affairs S. Jaishankar hailed that direct talks with Iran are crucial for restarting shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. President Donald Trump warned that NATO faces a “very bad” future if allies do not assist in reopening the critical oil route, which Iran has effectively closed. Japan’s Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi announced that Japan will not send naval vessels to escort ships in West Asia, following Trump’s call for allies to protect tankers. Australia echoed Japan’s stance. Trump also urged NATO and China to help reopen the Strait as major economies began releasing oil reserves to prevent supply disruptions. Meanwhile, India’s Foreign Ministry stated that there have been no discussions with the U.S. regarding assistance for ships in the Strait. U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer mentioned that the U.K.’s plan with allies to reopen the Strait will not involve NATO.
Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump accused Iran of using artificial intelligence as a “disinformation weapon” to misrepresent its wartime successes and support.
The Israeli military announced that it had begun what it termed “limited ground operations” against Hezbollah in southern Lebanon. According to Lebanon’s State-run National News Agency, an Israeli airstrike early that day on a residence in the southern village of Kfar Sir resulted in one fatality. Additionally, a drone strike on oil facilities along the UAE’s eastern coast ignited a fire, as Iran continued its series of attacks on Gulf nations. Israel expanded its military presence in southern Lebanon, deploying troops to engage Hezbollah positions as it escalated its offensive against the Iran-backed militant group.
Union Civil Aviation Minister K. Rammohan Naidu informed the Rajya Sabha that Indian airlines have cancelled 4,335 flights and foreign carriers 1,187 flights so far in the wake of the West Asia conflict. Later, India evacuated over 550 of its nationals from Iran through Armenia, with External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar thanking Yerevan for facilitating the land-border transit.
The Israeli military says it has destroyed an estimated 70% of Iran’s missile launchers during the first two weeks of the war.
Day 18
Israel killed two senior Iranian security officials in a major blow to the Islamic Republic’s leadership as it faces its greatest test in decades. Iran responded on Wednesday (March 18, 2026) with fresh missile and drone attacks targeting Israel and several Gulf Arab neighbours.
Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran’s Supreme National Security Council, was regarded as one of the country’s most influential figures following the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in an airstrike on the opening day of the conflict, was killed in an overnight Israeli strike. Israel earlier announced it had killed Basij commander Gholamreza Soleimani in an air strike,
Meanwhile, Iran launched a barrage of missiles towards Israel, triggering air-raid sirens across central parts of the country and loud explosions in Tel Aviv. Israel’s emergency medical service, Magen David Adom, said two people were killed in Ramat Gan, east of Tel Aviv.
As oil prices climbed, U.S. President Donald Trump said he had called on around half a dozen countries to deploy warships to ensure the safe passage of commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz. Mr. Trump complained that Washington was receiving little support “despite the fact that almost every country strongly agreed with what we are doing”, adding that Iran must not be allowed to obtain a nuclear weapon. The United States military said it had struck Iranian missile sites along the country’s coastline near the Strait of Hormuz, citing the threat posed by Iranian anti-ship missiles to international shipping.
Day 19
Announcing the development, Israel Katz said that “significant surprises are expected throughout this day on all the fronts,” without providing further details. Israel also reportedly attacked an Iranian offshore natural gas field on Wednesday (March 18, 2026) as the war escalated pressure on the region’s economic lifeblood: energy.
Mr. Khatib’s death comes after Israel killed senior Iranian security official Ali Larijani and Gholamreza Soleimani, the head of the Revolutionary Guard’s all-volunteer Basij force.
Earlier, Iran’s judiciary said an “attack” had struck southern Iran, resulting in civilian casualties as well as deaths and injuries among judicial staff. The judiciary’s Mizan news agency reported that the strike hit a judicial facility in Larestan county in Fars province.
Meanwhile, Iran is set to hold funerals for Mr. Larijani and Mr. Soleimani on Wednesday (March 18, 2026), a day after they were killed in Israeli airstrikes. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the assassination of Mr. Larijani would not destabilise the country’s political system, even as the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps vowed “definite revenge”.
Day 20
As the escalating conflict in West Asia entered its 20th consecutive day, energy prices surged on Thursday (March 19, 2026) and stocks sank as Iran attacked several energy facilities in the Gulf and warned of further retaliation following an attack on one of its key gas fields. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said that Iran is being “decimated” and it is unclear who is in charge, even as the Islamic Republic kept up attacks on Gulf oil and gas targets that have jolted global markets.
Energy prices soared and stocks sank as Iran attacked several Gulf energy facilities and warned of more in retaliation for a strike on one of its key gas fields. Brent crude rose above $115 a barrel after Tehran threatened to target regional installations in response to an Israeli strike on a site linked to the South Pars gas field, which Iran shares with Qatar. Concerns over global energy supplies pushed European gas prices up by more than 30%, while Brent crude gains stood at about 7%.
Meanwhile, a ship burnt off the coast of the United Arab Emirates and another was damaged off Qatar, highlighting the persistent risks to shipping amid Iran’s control over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran’s military on Thursday (March 19, 2026) reiterated its threat to destroy the region’s energy infrastructure if its facilities were attacked again amid the U.S.-Israel war with the Islamic Republic. At the same time, Iranian lawmakers proposed imposing tolls and taxes on ships transiting the strategic Strait of Hormuz, local media reported on Thursday (March 19, 2026).
Israel said its attack on Iran’s South Pars gas facilities on Wednesday (March 18, 2026) was coordinated with the United States but was unlikely to be repeated, according to three Israeli officials who spoke on Thursday (March 19, 2026).
U.S. President Donald Trump said in a social media post that Washington “knew nothing about this particular attack”, which has prompted widespread Iranian strikes on energy installations across the Gulf. Earlier, Mr. Trump had threatened to destroy Iran’s South Pars gas field if further attacks were carried out against Qatar’s main gas plant. He later confirmed that Israel had struck the South Pars field but but said the U.S. “knew nothing” of the attack, which spurred Iran to launch an attack on Qatar’s Ras Laffan facility.
Day 21
U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday (March 20, 2026) said that the United States was considering “winding down” military operations against Iran but was not seeking a ceasefire with the Islamic republic.
Meanwhile, Iran’s new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei meanwhile claimed in a message to mark the first day of the Persian New Year to have delivered a “dizzying blow” to enemies in the three-week-old West Asia war.
Fresh blasts hit Tehran on Friday (March 20, 2026) and Israel accused Iran of attacking holy sites in Jerusalem after a strike left a crater in the Old City near the Al-Aqsa Mosque, Western Wall and Church of the Holy Sepulchre.
With limited information emerging from Iran, the extent of damage to its military, nuclear or energy infrastructure since the conflict began on February 28 remained unclear. However, Iran has demonstrated that it retains the capacity to launch attacks that have disrupted oil supplies and unsettled the global economy, pushing up food and fuel prices well beyond West Asia.
In India, the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas said during a briefing on Friday (March 20, 2026) that the increase in petrol prices had been restricted to the premium variant of fuel. The price hike was in the range of ₹2 to ₹3 a litre, while regular petrol remained unaffected. Joint Secretary Sujata Sharma said the liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) situation could become a concern but was not yet at a point where domestic supply would be affected. She added that the government was sourcing cargoes from all available markets.
Earlier, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said on Friday (March 20, 2026) that U.S.-Israeli strikes had killed their spokesman, Ali Mohammad Naini. Meanwhile, drone attacks caused a fire at Kuwait’s Mina Al-Ahmadi oil refinery, state media reported, as authorities in several Gulf countries said they were responding to attacks originating from Iran.
Day 22
On the 22nd day of the U.S.-Iran-Israel war, Prime Minister Narendra Modi on Saturday (March 21, 2026) spoke to Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian over the phone and condemned attacks on critical infrastructure in West Asia, saying such actions threaten regional stability and disrupt global supply chains. Extending Id and Nowruz greetings, the Prime Minister also appreciated Iran’s continued support in ensuring the safety and security of Indian nationals in the country.
Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz has warned that the United States and Israel will intensify their strikes on Iran in the coming week, beginning Sunday (March 22, 2026) after U.S. President Donald Trump said he was considering “winding down” military operations in the Mideast even as the United States announced it was sending more warships and Marines to the region and Iran threatened to attack tourist sites worldwide.
The mixed messages came after another climb in oil prices plunged the U.S. stock market, and was followed by a Trump administration announcement that it will lift sanctions on Iranian oil loaded on ships, a move aimed at wrangling soaring fuel prices.
Also, the death toll has risen to more than 1,300 people in Iran, more than 1,000 people in Lebanon, 15 in Israel and 13 U.S. military members in the region. Millions of people in Lebanon and Iran have been displaced.
Meanwhile, the United States and Israel struck Iran’s Natanz nuclear facility, Iran’s Atomic Energy Organisation said. “Following the criminal attacks by the United States and the usurping Zionist regime against our country, the Natanz enrichment complex was targeted this morning,” the organisation said in a statement carried by the Tasnim news agency. It added that no leakage of radioactive material was reported in the area in central Iran.
Day 23
Israel’s Defence Minister Israel Katz on Sunday (March 22, 2026) said that he and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had instructed the military to speed up the demolition of Lebanese homes in “frontline villages” to eliminate threats to Israeli communities. He said the armed forces had also been ordered to immediately destroy all bridges over Lebanon’s Litani river, which he claimed were being used for “terrorist activity”.
Israel said rocket fire from Lebanon killed one person on Sunday (March 22, 2026). Hezbollah said it had attacked Israeli soldiers in the country’s north, marking the first fatality in Israel due to fire originating from Lebanon since the war began.
The Strait of Hormuz remains open to all shipping except vessels linked to “Iran’s enemies”, Iran’s representative to the United Nations maritime agency said on Sunday (March 22, 2026). His remarks came after United States President Donald Trump threatened to target Iranian power plants if the waterway was not kept “fully open” within 48 hours.
Meanwhile, Iran’s Parliament Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf said critical infrastructure and energy facilities across West Asia could be “irreversibly destroyed” if Iranian power plants were attacked. In comments posted on X on Sunday (March 22, 2026), he warned that regional infrastructure would become “legitimate targets” should Iran’s facilities be hit, and that its retaliation would increase the price of oil for a while.
Day 24
U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday (March 23, 2026) extended the deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, saying Washington would refrain from striking Iranian power plants for another five days. Mr. Trump said U.S. envoys had been in touch with a “respected” Iranian leader and claimed that Iran wants “to make a deal.”
Iranian officials, however, denied any such negotiations and declared that the American President had back down following “following Iran’s firm warning”.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on Monday (March 23, 2026) said that he spoke with Mr. Trump, during which the U.S. President suggested that recent U.S. and Israeli military gains in Iran could be translated into a negotiated agreement that safeguards Israel’s interests.
Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei indicated that messages had been received from “some friendly countries indicating a U.S. request for negotiations aimed at ending the war,” according to the official IRNA news agency.
Following a series of rapidly shifting announcements, countries in the region expressed relief after Mr. steped back from his threat to target Iranian power infrastructure. Tehran had earlier warned of deploying naval mines in the Gulf and targeting power and water facilities across the region in retaliation, raising fears of an escalation that could have deepened an already severe energy crisis.
Day 25
Even as U.S. President Donald Trump spoke of “very good” talks with an unnamed Iranian official after abruptly shelving plans for fresh attacks, Iranian media reported on Tuesday (March 24, 2026) that Israeli and U.S. strikes had targeted two gas facilities and a pipeline. The reports came hours after Mr. Trump stepped back from his threat to strike Iran’s power infrastructure.
“As part of the ongoing attacks carried out by the Zionist and American enemy, the gas administration building and the gas pressure regulation station on Kaveh Street in Isfahan were targeted,” the Fars news agency reported.
The facilities in central Iran were “partially damaged”, Fars said, adding that it was the only Iranian news outlet to carry the report.
According to the news agency, a separate attack targeted the gas pipeline of the Khorramshahr power plant in southwest Iran.
Meanwhile, a series of strikes hit Beirut’s southern suburbs early on Tuesday (March 24, 2026), marking the first such attack on the Hezbollah stronghold in several days. The Israeli military said it had captured two members of the Iran-backed group in southern Lebanon.
The Israeli military also said it was striking Hezbollah infrastructure in Beirut after issuing evacuation warnings for residents of the southern suburbs. The Israeli Army’s Arabic-language spokesperson, Ella Waweya, said the “battle against Hezbollah had only just begun”.
Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump on Tuesday (March 24, 2026) said he had sent a peace proposal to Iran, expressing optimism about ending nearly a month of hostilities. His remarks came as Tehran announced it would allow “non-hostile” oil vessels to pass through the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz.
The same day, Mr. Trump held a telephone conversation with Prime Minister Narendra Modi to discuss the unfolding U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran. This was the first contact between the two leaders since the war began on February 28, 2026. Separately, Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said Islamabad was prepared to host negotiations aimed at halting the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, amid growing speculation about Pakistan’s potential role as a mediator.
Even as diplomatic signals emerged, Iran launched a fresh volley of missiles at Israel on Tuesday (March 24, 2026) causing damage and injuries in Tel Aviv. Tel Aviv Mayor Ron Huldai said a building in an upscale neighbourhood had suffered a “direct strike”, underscoring the continuing volatility despite talk of possible negotiations to end the three-week-old conflict.
The Pentagon is expected to deploy thousands of troops from the elite 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East, two people familiar with the matter told Reuters, adding to the region’s growing military presence even as the Trump administration seeks talks with Tehran. Earlier in the day, Israel carried out a series of strikes on Beirut’s southern suburbs, the first attack on the Hezbollah stronghold in days. The Israeli military said it had captured two members of the Iran-backed group in southern Lebanon.
Day 26
Iran has received a 15-point American ceasefire proposal for the Iran war through intermediaries from Pakistan, officials in Islamabad said on Wednesday (March 25, 2026). The proposal was conveyed even as Washington began moving paratroopers to West Asia to reinforce a contingent of U.S. Marines already en route to the region.
The U.S. proposal offered a ceasefire and sanctions relief in return for Tehran abandoning its nuclear programme and reopening the Strait of Hormuz. According to state-run Press TV, Iran has laid down five conditions to end the war, which the U.S. and Israel launched on February 28, 2026, causing the assassination of Iran Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and several other leaders.
Iran’s military, however, dismissed the diplomatic efforts and carried out further attacks on Israel and across the Persian Gulf region, including an assault that sparked a huge fire at Kuwait International Airport, sending black smoke billowing into the sky.
The Israeli military said on Wednesday (March 25,2026) that it had struck a naval cruise missile production facility operating under Iran’s Ministry of Defence in Tehran. Lebanese state media reported that Israeli strikes killed at least six people in a town and a Palestinian refugee camp near the southern city of Sidon, and three others in another nearby town.
Meanwhile, Iran said it had fired cruise missiles at the aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln. A statement carried by Iranian state television warned of additional launches when ships from the carrier strike group come within range. The Iranian Navy, citing its chief Admiral Shahram Irani, said the carrier group’s movements were being “constantly monitored” and that the fleet would face strong retaliation once it entered the range of Iran’s missile systems.
The Israeli Defense Forces said in a Telegram post it had launched a wave of strikes targeting infrastructure across Tehran. The semi-official Iranian SNN News Agency said the strikes hit a residential area in the city, with rescuers searching the rubble.
In India, responding to the questions raised by the Opposition regarding the reported central role played by Pakistan in mediating between the U.S. and Iran, External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar on Wednesday (March 25, 2026) termed the neighbouring country a “ dalal” and said that it had been playing this role since 1981.
Day 27
Iran’s Parliament is working on a bill to formalise fees that the country is reported to be levying on certain vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, local media have reported. The Fars and Tasnim news agencies, both considered close to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, quoted lawmaker Mohammadreza Rezaei Kouchi as saying that “Parliament was pursuing a proposal to formally codify Iran’s sovereignty, control, and oversight over the Strait of Hormuz, while also creating a revenue through the collection of transit fees.”
Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Thursday (March 27, 2026) Donald Trump said he will delay a threatened strike on Iran’s energy infrastructure and extend his deadline for Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz until April 6, saying talks to end the war are “going very well.”
The move marks a pullback from Mr. Trump’s earlier warning that the U.S. would target Iran’s energy plants if the crucial shipping lane remained closed. Iran has threatened to retaliate against regional infrastructure, including desalination facilities, if the U.S. follows through.
Mr. Trump’s announcement on social media came after U.S. stocks fell sharply and oil prices rose, as doubt took over again on Wall Street about a possible end to the war.
Also, Israeli authorities said he was directly responsible for restricting movement through the Strait of Hormuz since the outbreak of hostilities. Iran did not immediately acknowledge Mr. Tangsiri’s killing.
Iran and the United States appeared to be at a stalemate on Thursday (March 25, 2026), with both sides adopting hardened positions on negotiations, raising the risk of further escalation in the West Asia conflict. Sirens sounded across parts of Israel, warning of incoming Iranian missile barrages, while Gulf countries reportedly took steps to intercept projectiles. Reports also spoke of heavy strikes on Tehran and other Iranian cities.
Day 28
In a video address to the United Nations Human Rights Council, Abbas Araghchi said that the United States’ contradictory remarks aimed at justifying their crime could not, in any manner, elude their responsibility,” describing the February 28, 2026 attack as “a war crime and a crime against humanity”.
“The bombing of the Shajareh Tayyebeh Elementary School in Minab evoked a visceral horror,” UN rights chief Volker Turk told the United Nations Human Rights Council, stressing that “the onus is on those who carried out the attack to investigate it promptly, impartially, transparently and thoroughly”.
Meanwhile, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards urged civilians across the region to avoid areas close to United States forces. U.S. President Donald Trump has extended the deadline for Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz to April 6.
In India, Prime Minister Narendra Modi on Friday (March 27, 2026) interacted with the Chief Ministers of all States, barring those which are going to polls, to review preparedness and planning in the context of the West Asia conflict. Chief Ministers of poll-bound States did not take part in the meeting as the Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is in force.
Published - March 04, 2026 06:14 pm IST

articles.title: Tehran accuses U.S. of 'calculated' assault on school
articles.summary: U.S. President Donald Trump initially suggested that Iran itself may have been responsible for the attack on the Iranian elementary school in Minab — despite Iran not having Tomahawk missiles.
articles.full_text:
Iran's Foreign Minister on Friday (March 27, 2026) branded a deadly strike on an Iranian school on the first day of the West Asian war as a "calculated" U.S. assault.
Iran-Israel war LIVE updates: March 27, 2026
Addressing an urgent debate in the United Nations Human Rights Council focused on the February 28 strike on an Iranian elementary school in Minab, Abbas Araghchi said "more than 175 students and teachers were slaughtered in cold blood" in a "calculated, phased assault".
The strike, he said in a video address, "was a war crime and a crime against humanity, one that demands unequivocal condemnation by all, and unambiguous accountability for the culprits".
UN rights chief Volker Turk, meanwhile, said the bombing evoked "visceral horror", insisting upon the need for "justice", in a video address to the council.
Mohaddeseh Falahat, the mother of two children who died in the attack, also spoke to the council by video, calling for the diplomats in Geneva "not to let this tragedy be forgotten".
"No mother is prepared to hear the words: 'Your child is not coming back'," she said, in an emotional address.
'Deliberate and intentional'
The attack happened on February 28, on the day the United States and Israel launched the war with attacks across Iran, with Tehran in turn striking targets in Israel and Gulf nations.
A U.S. Tomahawk cruise missile hit the elementary school due to a targeting mistake, according to the preliminary findings of a U.S. military investigation reported by The New York Times.
The newspaper said the U.S. military was bombing an adjacent Iranian base, of which the school building was formerly a part, and target coordinates were set using outdated data.
U.S. President Donald Trump initially suggested that Iran itself may have been responsible — despite Iran not having Tomahawk missiles.
UN rights chief Turk stressed that "the onus is on those who carried out the attack to investigate it promptly, impartially, transparently and thoroughly".
"Senior U.S. officials have said the strike is under investigation," he said, calling for its findings to be made public.
Mr. Araghchi did not wait for the results of the probe to attribute blame.
"At a time when the American-Israeli aggressors, in their own assertions, possess the most advanced technologies and the highest-precision military and data systems, no one can believe that the attack on the school was anything other than deliberate and intentional," he told the council.
'Atrocity'
"This atrocity cannot be justified, cannot be concealed, and must not be met with silence and indifference," the Minister said.
"The United States' contradictory remarks aimed at justifying their crime could not, in any manner, elude their responsibility," he insisted.
Friday's discussion, at the request of Iran, China and Cuba, marked the second urgent debate before the rights council this week, focused on the war in West Asia.
On Wednesday (March 25, 2026), a debate requested by Bahrain on behalf of the Gulf Cooperation Council and Jordan, considered Iran's strikes on countries across the Gulf region and their impact on civilians.
Following that debate, the 47-member council approved by consensus a resolution condemning Iran's "egregious attacks" on its Gulf neighbours, calling for swift "reparation" to all victims of its strikes.
No draft resolution was discussed during Friday's debate.
Published - March 27, 2026 05:12 pm IST

articles.title: Ukraine, Saudi Arabia agreed to key defence cooperation: Zelenskyy
articles.summary: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, in a social media post on Friday (March 27), said the document "lays the foundation for future contracts, technological cooperation, and investment", providing no further details.
articles.full_text:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, during his visit to Saudi Arabia on Friday (March 27, 2026), said the two countries have agreed to "an important arrangement" on defence cooperation.
Mr. Zelenskyy, in a social media post on Friday (March 27), said the document "lays the foundation for future contracts, technological cooperation, and investment", providing no further details.
Drone combat experts from Ukraine, with over four years of experience in fending off Iran-manufactured drones used during the Russian offensive, are helping five Gulf countries to counter Tehran's attacks, according to Mr. Zelenskyy.
The Ukraine President said he would meet later in the day with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.
Published - March 27, 2026 04:12 pm IST

articles.title: Government sanctions ₹31,000 crore to fence Myanmar border
articles.summary: Home Minister Amit Shah says fencing work has been completed on 30 km of the border, which, he termed the root cause of the ethnic violence in Manipur
articles.full_text:
The Union government has approved ₹31,000 crore to fence the Myanmar border, a senior Home Ministry official said on Wednesday.
The 1,643-km Myanmar border runs along the States of Arunachal Pradesh (520 km), Nagaland (215 km), Manipur (398 km) and Mizoram (510 km).
On Tuesday, Union Home Minister Amit Shah said fencing work had been completed on 30 km of the border, which, he termed the root cause of the ethnic violence in Manipur.
The Cabinet Committee on Security had, in principle, approved the construction of border fencing and roads along the 1,643-km international border between India and Myanmar at an approximate cost of ₹31,000 crore, the official said.
About 10 km of fencing has already been completed near Moreh and work is under way to fence another 21 km of the border in other areas of Manipur.
The Union Home Ministry earlier this year scrapped the Free Movement Regime (FMR) along the Myanmar border which allows people residing close to the border to venture 16 km into each other’s territory without any documents.
The official said two battalions of the Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF) along with 200 companies of other central armed police forces (CAPF), around 22,000 personnel had been deployed in Manipur. Other than this, the Manipur police and the Army were deployed in the State.
“Continuous dialogues are being held with both Meitei and Kuki-Zo communities to restore peace in the region as soon as possible,” said the official.
At least 237 people have been killed in the ethnic violence between the tribal Kuki-Zo and the Meitei people since May 3, 2023. More than 60,000 people have been displaced from their homes.
Published - September 18, 2024 09:29 pm IST

articles.title: U.S.-Israel-Iran war: List of countries allowed to cross the Strait of Hormuz
articles.summary: Global oil and gas prices have surged after Iran virtually blocked the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow shipping lane between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, that handles roughly 20 per cent of global oil and LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas).
articles.full_text:
Amid the escalating war in the Middle East, Iran has given its explicit permission to some countries, allowing their ships to safely pass through the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
Starting February 28, the U.S. and Israel launched joint strikes against Iran, which in turn retaliated, spreading the war to the entire Gulf region. The Strait of Hormuz is a strategically important choke point for the world’s energy supplies.
Global oil and gas prices have surged after Iran virtually blocked the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow shipping lane between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, that handles roughly 20% of global oil and LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas).
However, Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi said that the U.S., Israel and some Gulf States involved in the current war will not be allowed to pass through the strait.
Also read: Israel-Iran war LIVE updates
Here’s the list of countries that managed to secure safe passage for their vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.
India
Iran has said it will allow Indian ships to safely pass through the Strait of Hormuz. India has been included in Iran’s list of ‘friendly countries’ in the current conflict.
Since the U.S. and Israel started the conflict with Iran on February 28, at least four ships flying the Indian flag: Jag Vasant, Pine Gas, Shivalik, and Nanda Devi, have passed through the strait.
China
A majority of the ships that have made it through in recent weeks headed east, out of the Gulf. Among them, China accounted for 10%, counted by ownership or flag registration
Thailand
A Thai oil tanker has safely passed through the Strait of Hormuz following diplomatic coordination between Thailand and Iran, and was not required to pay to escape the blockade, a Thai official and the oil major that owns the vessel said.
The Bangchak Corporation-owned tanker crossed the strategic waterway on March 25, 2026 after successful talks between Thai foreign minister Sihasak Phuangketkeow and Iran’s ambassador to Thailand.
Russia
Iran has explicitly authorised Russian vessels to use the Strait of Hormuz for commercial shipping. While Russia has official permission, most global shipping through the strait has seen a 95% drop since the outbreak of hostilities in late February 2026.
Pakistan
Between March 14 and 15, a Pakistan-bound tanker successfully crossed the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first such transit since the war began. However, another Pakistan-bound tanker was turned away the following week by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) for not adhering to legal protocols. As of the latest announcement from Mr. Araghchi, Pakistan is among the list of ‘friendly countries’ that are allowed to pass through the strait.
Iraq
After weeks of negotiations between Baghdad and Tehran, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi officially designated Iraq as a ‘friendly nation,’ granting Iraqi-owned vessels safe passage through the Strait.
Only about 150 vessels, including tankers and container ships, have transited since March 1, according to Lloyd’s List Intelligence shipping information firm. That’s a little more than one day’s normal traffic before the war. Iran’s Kharg Island terminal loaded 1.6 million barrels in March — largely unchanged from prewar monthly loading totals, according to data and analytic firm Kpler. Most of the customers are small, private refineries in China that don’t care about U.S. sanctions.
(With Agencies Inputs)
Published - March 27, 2026 11:09 am IST

articles.title: IDF chief warns of potential collapse due to manpower shortage: report
articles.summary: Gen. Zamir’s comments came at a time when Israel is fighting wars on different fronts
articles.full_text:
Israel Defence Forces (IDF) chief Lt. Gen. Eyal Zamir told a security cabinet meeting that the military “is going to collapse in on itself” amid a growing manpower shortage, Israeli media reported.
“I am raising 10 red flags in front of you. Right now, the IDF needs a conscription law, a reserve duty law, and a law to extend mandatory service,” Gen. Zamir told Ministers, according to a report Channel 13. “Before long, the IDF will not be ready for its routine missions and the reserve system will not last,” he said. The Times of Israel also reported his comments.
Gen. Zamir’s comments came at a time when Israel is fighting wars on different fronts. It has been carrying out extensive air strikes on Iran since February 28. The IDF has also invaded Lebanon to fight Hezbollah and occupy more Lebanese territory in the south. In the West Bank, where settler violence against Palestinians is on the rise, the IDF has deployed thousands troops.
On Thursday (March 26, 2026), the IDF announced that an Israeli soldier was killed and four others wounded in a Hezbollah anti-tank missile attack. Since the latest fighting with Hezbollah began on March 2, at least four Israeli troops have been killed. Hezbollah says it is resisting the Israeli invasion in the south, and has launched hundreds of rockets into Israel in the past three weeks. According to Lebanese authorities, at least 1,070 were killed by Israeli strikes since March 2. More than 1 million people have been displaced.
Israel’s Opposition Leader Yair Lapid blamed the government of Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for “sending the army into a multi-front war without a strategy, without sufficient resources, and with too few soldiers.”
“This time, the government will not be able to say, ‘I didn’t know.’ This is the chief of staff they appointed, and they will not be able to politicise him and shift the blame onto him,” Mr. Lapid said.
Published - March 27, 2026 03:50 pm IST

articles.title: Israel launches new wave of strikes on Iran with no sign of diplomatic breakthrough
articles.summary: Israel’s attack on targets “in the heart of Tehran” came after the country said it would be “intensifying” its strikes on Iranian weapons production facilities
articles.full_text:
Israel launched a wave of strikes on Iran on early Friday (March 27, 2026) ahead of a planned United Nations Security Council meeting to discuss attacks on Iranian civilian infrastructure, while Iran and the United States appeared at a diplomatic impasse, setting the stage for more potential escalation as the first month of the war in West Asia neared its end.
Israel’s attack on Friday (March 27, 2026) on targets “in the heart of Tehran” came after the country said it would be “intensifying” its strikes on Iranian weapons production facilities, but there was no immediate information on what was hit.
Smoke also rose over Beirut, although Israel did not immediately report hitting the Lebanese capital, while air raid sirens sounded in Israel as the military said it was working to intercept Iranian missiles. Iran kept up its attacks on its Gulf Arab neighbours, with incoming drones and missiles reported in both Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates.
The U.S. has been pushing Iran to start talks on a 15-point proposal for a ceasefire but, at the same time has ordered thousands more troops to the region, possibly in preparation for a military attempt to wrest the Strait of Hormuz from Iran’s tight grip.
After Wall Street’s worst day since the war began, Asian shares mostly fell Friday (March 27, 2026) over growing doubts about the chances of de-escalation. Oil prices rose again, with Brent crude, the international standard, at $107 a barrel in morning trading, up more than 45 per cent since Israel and the U.S. attacked Iran on February 28 to start the war.
U.S. pushing diplomatic solution but sending more troops to the region
Iran’s stranglehold on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has caused growing concerns of a global energy crisis and appears part of a strategy to get the U.S. to back down by roiling the world economy. A Gulf Arab bloc said Thursday (March 26, 2026) that Iran is now exacting tolls from ships to ensure their safe passage through the waterway.
Trump envoy Steve Witkoff said Washington has delivered a 15-point “action list” to Iran for a possible ceasefire, using Pakistan as an intermediary. The list includes restrictions on Iran's nuclear programme and re-opening the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has rejected the U.S. offer and put forth its own five-point proposal, which includes reparations and recognition of its sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.
As the diplomatic efforts went on, a group of U.S. ships drew closer to the region with some 2,500 Marines. Also, at least 1,000 paratroopers from the 82nd Airborne — trained to land in hostile territory to secure key territory and airfields — have been ordered to the region.
As American and Israeli attacks on Iran continued, the UN Security Council scheduled closed consultation on Iran for Friday (March 27, 2026) in New York, according to two UN diplomats who spoke on condition of anonymity because the meeting is not public.
They added that Russia had asked for the meeting on U.S.-Israeli attacks on civilian infrastructure in the country and that the United States, which holds the Security Council presidency, had scheduled it.
Deaths continue to climb, primarily in Iran and Lebanon
Since the war began, more than 1,900 people have been killed in Iran, according to the Health Ministry.
Eighteen people have died in Israel, while at least three Israeli soldiers have also been killed in Lebanon. At least 13 American troops have been killed. Four people in the occupied West Bank and 20 in Gulf Arab states have also died.
Authorities said more than 1,100 people have died in Lebanon. In Iraq, where Iranian-supported militia groups have entered the conflict, 80 members of the security forces have been killed.
Published - March 27, 2026 12:54 pm IST

articles.title: ‘Trade deal with India not far-off, but there are still gaps’: U.S. official
articles.summary: One of the gaps in India-U.S. discussions, as per the U.S. official, is around pulses, with India seeking to safeguard the market for this agricultural commodity and the U.S. wanting greater access to it
articles.full_text:
Washington DC
The U.S. and India are not “far off” from finalizing an interim trade deal, but need to bridge some negotiating gaps, such as over discussions on pulses and the staging of tariff reductions, according to a U.S. official.
“We’re not that far off from finalizing the interim trade deal, but some gaps remain,” the official told The Hindu in recent days.
Officials in Washington are working out the nitty-gritty of trade agreements following the U..S Supreme Court’s February 20 ruling that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), which was the basis for President Donald Trump’s ‘reciprocal tariffs’, was unlawfully applied. The administration is now working towards re-establishing the reciprocal tariffs using other legislative tools, including ‘Special’ 301 trade investigations conducted by the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative’s (USTR).
One of the gaps in India-U.S. discussions, as per the U.S. official, is around pulses, with India seeking to safeguard the market for this agricultural commodity and the U.S. wanting greater access to it. Politically, agriculture is a deeply sensitive issue for both countries, with the administration in DC and government in New Delhi not singing from the same song sheet— or, more literally, ‘fact sheet’.
Last month, the White House put out a ‘fact sheet’– including the term “certain pulses” on a list of items that, it claimed, India would cut tariffs on. This and other discrepancies with the joint statement issued by Washington and New Delhi had created a political furore in India. The White House quietly reissued the ‘fact sheet’ – a political statement, rather than a statement of fact– this time omitting the reference to pulses and correcting certain other discrepancies.
However, the U.S. side still appears to be pushing for market access on pulses. Additionally, The Hindu has learned that Washington is seeking reduced staging (i.e., the speeding up of a phased reduction in tariffs) and this is currently one of the gaps in negotiating positions and is under discussion.
Conversations The Hindu has had in recent days suggest that right now, however, trade officials in Washington are not focused on closing the gaps in trade deals but are busy conducting Special 301 investigations launched in March against tens of countries, including India, ostensibly for excess capacity in manufacturing and forced labour.
Farmers’ pulse: On India and its demand for pulses
While the U.S. did sign a trade deal since the latest set of 301s was launched, this was with Ecuador, (on March 13), a country with an economy at least 30 times smaller than India and whose bilateral trade with the U.S. is a fraction of U..S-India bilateral trade.
These probes— which are conducted annually on different countries— will enable the imposition of tariffs on top of the universal tariff rate of 10% , imposed on February 24 using Section 122 of the U.S.’s Trade Act, following the Supreme Court ruling. Section 122 tariffs, applied temporarily when there is a balance of payments issue, can be applied for no more than 150 days and cannot exceed 15% as per law.
Mr. Trump had said in February that he would increase the universal 10% rate to 15%. However, this has not happened and officials in DC appeared unsure whether it would happen at all. A question to the White House on the timing of any such hike went unanswered. The USTR also did not provide information on possible rate hike, when approached by The Hindu.
Hiking this universal tariff to 15% would also complicate Washington’s agreements with countries that had reciprocal tariffs of less than 15%. Additionally, the White House has been focused on Iran in terms of foreign policy and the Department of Homeland Security shutdown, domestically.
Meanwhile, countries are busy with another part of the trade agenda: the World Trade Organization’s 14th ministerial conference in Cameroon March 26-29. Among the contentious topics is trade in e-commerce, where Indian and American positions are not aligned. India has for long wanted an end to the moratorium on tariffs on various forms of e-commerce (software downloads, digital music, etc.) while the U.S. would like to make this permanent.
Published - March 27, 2026 04:08 am IST

articles.title: The recipe behind China’s EV boom: Technology over price
articles.summary: XPeng founder He Xiaopeng says intelligent driving and in-house innovation are powering China’s EV boom, even as competition intensifies, tech firms enter the space, and new bets on robotics and flying cars reshape mobility
articles.full_text:
Since the launch of its first EV model in early 2019, XPeng Motors has emerged as a major domestic player making waves in China, with its AI-powered smart driving cars increasingly ubiquitous on the streets of Beijing, as it looks to challenge the dominance of Chinese giant BYD, now the world’s largest EV maker, and overseas competitors such as Tesla. The company delivered 4.3 lakh units in 2025, doubling its sales. Its founder, entrepreneur He Xiaopeng, in an interview, explains the factors behind his company’s — and China’s — EV success.
Its founder, entrepreneur He Xiaopeng, in an interview, explains the factors behind his company’s — and China’s — EV success.
How do you view the recent rapid growth of both XPeng and China’s EV market as a whole?
One key reason for our [XPeng’s] success is the popularisation of high-level intelligent driving. We stick to full-stack, in-house R&D for intelligent driving, including intelligent driving hardware, free software, and frequent over-the-air updates, making premium intelligent driving accessible to all users. The industry has seen intense competition over the past two years. But long-term price-only competition erodes profits and innovation capacity. Meaningful competition lies in capabilities, technology and systems. It is not only about price.
China’s EV production exceeded 16 million (1.6 crore) units in 2025, with a penetration rate of 57%. Consumer choices are no longer a simple switch from fuel to electric, but an upgrade from ordinary cars to smart vehicles. I believe the industry will enter a deep elimination phase over the next 3-5 years, with a sharp reduction in the number of brands. International giants are accelerating electrification and smart transformation, intensifying global competition. Meanwhile, Chinese brands are expanding their first-mover advantages. Internal competition will shift from homogeneous cutthroat rivalry to differentiated breakthroughs. Healthy industrial competition lifts overall technological standards. Only by shifting from price competition to competition in innovation, capabilities and long-term value can China’s smart mobility industry achieve high-quality development and maintain global leadership. Our [Xpeng’s] internationalisation is accelerating steadily. In 2025, our overseas deliveries totalled 45,008 units, up 96%. Europe performed strongly with 22,787 units delivered, up 126% year-on-year.
It’s not just carmakers but tech companies getting into EVs in China; smartphone maker Xiaomi is an example. What do you see as the advantages and disadvantages they have when compared with traditional automakers?
Based on years of practice, we believe success in EVs relies on two core capabilities. One is long-term in-house development of R&D for core technologies, with sustained investment in intelligent driving, physical AI and large models. The other is user-centric technology deployment, turning cutting-edge tech into tangible, trustworthy daily experiences.
The two types of companies have different origins, genes, and paths, but they will eventually converge with no absolute superiority, but only phased differences. Many new automakers come from the Internet, building from software to hardware, with an inherent DNA of software-defined vehicles, AI-driven experiences, and treating cars as intelligent terminals from the start. Traditional automakers are building from hardware to software, leveraging decades of automotive manufacturing to add intelligent and technological capabilities.
First, the strengths I see are native AI and software thinking, short decision-chains, fast product iteration, deep understanding of user experience, and more sensitivity to frontier tech, whether autonomous driving, robots, or flying cars. Their innovation boundaries are wider. Their weakness lies in needing time to accumulate automotive manufacturing and supply chain management expertise. For automakers, they have a deep manufacturing heritage, mature production, quality control and supply chain capabilities. Their weakness lies in being slow to build software and AI talent systems. They need to improve their rapid iteration capabilities.
In China, we are also seeing a big push by many EV firms, including yours, into robotics…
Humanoid robots are at a strategic inflection point, moving from technical validation to large-scale commercialisation, similar to EVs a decade ago. Humanoid robots share a strong commonality with cars. In my view, embodied intelligence includes both humanoid robots and automobiles. In the next decade, cars will evolve into automotive robots, with an overlap of around 90% software and 50-60% in hardware. However, not all automakers can successfully transition to humanoid robots, as most rely on integrated supplier-based R&D. Success in the next decade requires in-house hardware and software R&D, cross-domain integration, and commercial capabilities. Only strong tech companies can capture this convergence of technological innovation and industry.
Chinese and American robots are leading the global first tier, each with unique strengths. Humanoid robots, as physical carriers of embodied intelligence, are at the cusp of an explosion similar to EVs a decade ago. China launched over 300 models last year, more than half the global total. Humanoid robot R&D is an extremely complex hardware-software engineering project. Xpeng has taken a forward-looking path: building a fully integrated intelligent agent with extreme human likeness in form and intelligence [called IRON]. IRON’s core advantages lie in its human-like design, and 2026 will be Xpeng’s first year of mass production. IRON is a high-level humanoid robot, a fully integrated intelligent agent with extreme human likeness in both form and intelligence — a global first. It’s designed from the inside out, with a human-like spine, bionic muscles, and fully wrapped flexible skin. By the end of 2026, Xpeng aims to achieve large-scale mass production of high-level humanoid robots, which will first serve commercial scenarios such as guidance, shopping assistance, and patrol.
What do you see as the potential for the “flying cars” market and what’s being called “the low altitude economy” in China?
I am encouraged by the mention of the low-altitude economy in the Government Work Report [China’s official policy document released on March 5, 2026]. It is evolving into a major new pillar of the economy and will rise further in five years. Between 2030 and 2040, it will transform daily life and work. Policymakers have a clear long-term vision, which I welcome greatly.
In 2026, Xpeng’s first flying car, the Land Aircraft Carrier, will enter mass production and delivery. In March, batch trial production and multi-aircraft test flights of the AeroHT Land Aircraft Carrier were completed. The AeroHT plant is the world’s first modern assembly line for large-scale flying car production, integrating aviation and automotive manufacturing to meet strict aviation standards. It has a planned annual capacity of 10,000 units, and an initial capacity of 5,000 units with one aircraft rolling off the line every 30 minutes at full production. That is 10 times the efficiency of traditional aviation manufacturing.
The low-altitude economy will unlock three 1 trillion RMB ($145 billion) markets. The first trillion layer is manufacturing of light aircraft, helicopters, drones; the second is investment in infrastructure including general airports, helipads and low-altitude networks; the third is tech services, air mobility and consumption upgrading driven by innovation. This includes personal entertainment and tourism — flight experiences, sightseeing and training — urban commuting, and public services, such as emergency medical rescue, highway accident response and high-rise evacuations.
Published - March 27, 2026 08:30 am IST

articles.title: ‘Someone asked if my book was influenced by Dhurandhar’: author Sarnath Banerjee
articles.summary: Banerjee’s new book, Absolute Jafar, is an India-Pakistan love story that unfolds across Delhi, Chicago, Karachi and Berlin
articles.full_text:
Have you ever stepped out of a cinema hall, wanting to run back and catch the next show of the same movie? That’s how one feels after reading Sarnath Banerjee’s latest graphic novel Absolute Jafar (HarperCollins India), a romance between Brighu from India and Mahrukh from Pakistan, which unfolds across Delhi, Chicago, Karachi and Berlin. The trauma that borders cause, moments of tenderness, wacky humour, and cinematic references pull one into the world that Banerjee has constructed.
The public curiosity about the cross-border relationship owes also to the fact that Banerjee was once married to Pakistani artist Bani Abidi. That said, tracking down autobiographical material instead of relishing how the author has transformed it into art is probably the worst way to read this book.
“Even if Absolute Jafar is about many things, such as childhood, the idea of home, relocation, fantasies of urban living, walking, music, clashing modernities, local universes, history of how a people felt at a certain time, the line of inquiry invariably boils down to the Indo-Pak aspect of it,” Banerjee laments. “Someone even asked me if my book, which I started writing seven years ago, was influenced by Dhurandhar,” he adds, referring to the recent Bollywood blockbuster about an Indian intelligence agent who infiltrates Pakistan to dismantle a terror network. Edited excerpts from an email conversation:
Q: How does it feel to write an India-Pakistan love story in the current political environment where diplomatic ties between the two countries are at an all-time low?
A: There has never been a golden age of an Indo-Pak love story. The relationship between these two countries has always been tenacious; marrying a Pakistani has never been easy, whether now or earlier. It is impossible to visit each other’s countries. Indo-Pak couples were never meant to live in India; eventually, they had to go to Dubai or Canada.
